root.narration: You are programming a helper robot that picks out your clothes each morning! Today you want the robot to check the weather and decide what you should wear. IF it is raining, THEN grab a raincoat. IF it is sunny, THEN grab sunglasses. How should you write this rule for the robot?

choices.text: IF raining THEN raincoat. IF sunny THEN sunglasses.
Always grab raincoat AND sunglasses no matter what.
Just grab whatever is closest. Do not check the weather.

outcome.headline: Smart Decision! Perfect Outfit!
outcome.explanation: You used IF/THEN! This is how computers make decisions. They check if something is true, and THEN they do something based on the answer. IF it is raining THEN wear a raincoat. The computer checks the weather (that is the IF part) and then picks the right clothes (that is the THEN part).
outcome.whatToRemember: IF/THEN lets a computer make decisions. It checks if something is true, then does something based on the answer.

keyTerms.definition: A rule that says: IF something is true, THEN do this action. It helps computers make decisions.

outcome.headline: You Will Not Get Wet... But Why the Sunglasses in the Rain?
outcome.explanation: Grabbing both every day means you are always prepared, but you are carrying extra stuff you do not need! A computer is smarter when it checks what is happening first and THEN decides. That way it only does what is needed.
outcome.whatToRemember: IF/THEN is better than always doing everything. It lets the computer be smart and only do what makes sense.

outcome.headline: Sunglasses in a Thunderstorm!
outcome.explanation: Without checking the weather, the robot just grabbed sunglasses on a rainy day! You are soaking wet! Computers need rules to make good decisions. Without IF/THEN, they cannot look at what is happening and choose the right action.
outcome.whatToRemember: Without IF/THEN, a computer cannot make smart choices. It needs to check what is happening before it decides what to do.
